channel.title: Recommendation System on DAXPY
channel.description: Recent content in Recommendation System on DAXPY

item.title: Scaling CF with Neural Networks and Features
item.description:
Scaling CF with Neural Networks and Features

Part 3/5 of Collaborative Filtering Collection

So far, we have discussed Neighborhood-Based Collaborative Filtering, Matrix Factorisation, Weighted Matrix Factorization and Logistic Matrix Factorization (part 1 and part 2). They all have the inherent flaw of “cold start” as they are built on top of interaction history. No interactions means no recommendations. New users and items are practically invisible to these algorithms.

But if we really peel out the layers of what we have built so far, it seems kinda funny. All the techniques so far has resulted in a vector per user and item. We have built a giant look up table !!

item.title: The "Zero" Problem
item.description:
The “Zero” Problem

Part 2/5 of Collaborative Filtering Collection

In part 1, we discussed predicting $P(click)$ using an interaction matrix. However, we concluded that traditional matrix factorization is not viable when the matrix contains empty cells.

Our approach should account for the uncertainty of probability of a click for those empty cells. So we pivot: instead of trying to factorize a full matrix, we treat this as an Optimisation Problem over the data we actually have.

item.title: Collaborative Filtering - The Foundations and Flaws
item.description:
Collaborative Filtering - The Foundations and Flaws

Part 1/5 of Collaborative Filtering Collection

In the world of recommendation-system, the main objective simple to state but hard to do:

Figure out which content to recommend to which user.

The easiest strategy is to show the most popular items and hope for the best. But can we do better?

This means figuring out which content a particular user is likely to enjoy. Not just what the crowd likes. That sounds straightforward until we hit two hard realities:
